segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an aerial view of a vast, rural landscape, transitioning into a prison corridor where a voice says 'Adelante'. A man in a red 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' t-shirt walks with guards, as a narrator states, 'We walked into the gangs.' He gestures toward rows of heavily tattooed inmates in white uniforms seated on the floor, who respond '¡Buenas tardes!' to a call, surrounded by masked officers. The narrator explains, 'They locked them all up. Two gangs that had been terrorizing everyone. Now they're all in one place.' The scene cuts to an El Salvador cityscape with a prominent volcano, then to a news montage showing Nayib Bukele’s 2019 presidential victory, including his swearing-in ceremony and public speeches. The narrator highlights, 'This is what the President of El Salvador did. He changed the entire country... from being the world's crime capital for decades, to becoming one of the safest countries in the world today. All because of this president. This is Nayib Bukele.' The narrator further details Bukele's Arab and Palestinian descent. The video then shows Bukele addressing a crowd with soldiers in formation, followed by a nighttime prison scene with inmates being led by officers in riot gear. The segment ends with Bukele speaking in Spanish at a podium with El Salvador flags, stating 'These criminals of parliament...' with Arabic subtitles translating his remarks about parliamentary criminals and the need to guarantee public safety, as the narrator concludes, 'Since taking office in 2019, he has transformed the country.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a news clip of Nayib Bukele, President of El Salvador, speaking at a podium with the Salvadoran flag, as a male voiceover translates his commitment to guaranteeing the safety of the people. The scene transitions to an aerial view of the 'CECOT - Centro de Confinamiento del Terrorismo' prison. A man in a red t-shirt, identified by his distinctive attire, stands outside the prison, gesturing expansively while stating, "This is Secot prison in El Salvador," and later adding, "It's one of the most secure prisons in the world, here in El Salvador," over soft female Spanish singing. The camera captures various angles of the prison exterior, including a guard and an arriving vehicle, before shifting to a first-person perspective from inside a van. The 'Man in red t-shirt' is visible in the back seat, engaging in conversation as the van drives along tree-lined roads, through rural landscapes, and into a cityscape with a prominent volcano, accompanied by car sounds and indistinct chatter. A different male voiceover mentions President Bukele's strong stance against gangs. The 'Man in red t-shirt' then explains, "Today we'll uncover the story of El Salvador and step inside Secot prison. We drove for an hour from the city to reach Secot," describing it as "one of the most heavily guarded prisons in the world." The segment concludes with a news montage showing armed soldiers during a state of emergency, with on-screen text "El Salvador records deadliest day in 30 years" and Arabic subtitles, as a male voiceover states, "In March 2022, El Salvador is under state of emergency," followed by another voiceover mentioning the declaration of a state of emergency. The final shot shows a high-security prison wall with a watchtower from a moving vehicle.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an aerial view of the CECOT prison, a large, modern facility with high concrete walls, barbed wire, and a watchtower, set against green hills and farmland. A male narrator introduces it as a new prison, opened in 2023, built specifically for 64,000 gang members and known as the prison for gangsters and terrorists, all set to tense, orchestral background music. The camera moves to ground level, showing the entrance gate with the sign 'CECOT - Centro de Confinamiento del Terrorismo' and armed guards in camouflage uniforms. The narrator emphasizes the high level of security. A man in a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back exits a van, accompanied by car sounds, and walks toward the gate, gesturing. Inside the prison, he walks through a brightly lit corridor, passing by a sign for 'PERSONAL ADMINISTRATIVO' and later 'CONTROL DE ACCESO'. He is heard speaking in a foreign language, translated by Arabic subtitles as "There's no signal around. All the area." and later "Hello, how are you?". He enters a security checkpoint area with metal detectors and X-ray machines, monitored by masked prison guards in blue uniforms. The narrator explains that media outlets were invited to broadcast the scene as a deterrent. A map of El Salvador briefly highlights the CECOT location before returning to the man inside the prison, where he is greeted by a guard who states, "Welcome. I'm the director of the Center of Confinement of Terrorism." The narrator concludes that the security measures are so tight it feels like going through an airport, with strict screening before entry, as the man places his belongings on a table for inspection, filmed by a camera operator.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with a low humming sound as a Man in a red t-shirt approaches a security checkpoint inside the CECOT prison, featuring a large 'Intercept' metal detector that glows red as he passes through. A narrator comments on the "advanced equipment," stating this prison is "not about rehabilitation" but "strictly for gang members." A Masked prison guard in a light blue uniform greets the man, gesturing him forward. The man hands over his phone and ID at a metal table for scanning. The narrator notes, "There are two more gates here," and identifies this as "the second checkpoint" and a "special inspection room." As the man proceeds through a sterile, brightly lit corridor, the narrator explains, "They check clothing and even socks; everything must be lightweight." Another Masked prison guard then conducts a thorough physical pat-down, checking his arms, back, and legs, and inspecting his white Nike sneakers, while a voice, possibly a guard, says "Comenzamos" (Let's begin) and "Vamos" (Let's go). The man remains calm and cooperative throughout. He continues walking, passing signs for 'CONTROL DE ACCESO 1' and 'SANITARIO', then exits into a covered outdoor area. Accompanied by a guard, he walks towards a door marked 'PRIVADOS DE LIBERTAD', re-entering the facility past a sign reading 'OBLIGATORIO USO DE ALCOHOL GEL'. The narrator observes, "We are entering another room now," and questions, "How many rooms do we have to go through before reaching the prisoners?" The entire process is filmed by a visible camera operator with a stabilizer, within the clean, institutional environment characterized by fluorescent lighting and minimal decoration.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the CECOT prison's single entrance, a long, covered driveway flanked by high walls and a metal roof, with signage for 'CECOT - Centro de Confinamiento del Terrorismo' and 'CONTROL DE ACCESO 1'. An informative male narrator states that "many years caused mourning and pain to our society," and describes the facility's "single entrance, entry and exit." The scene transitions to a conversation where the Man in red t-shirt, with a slightly accented voice, asks a Guard in black uniform, "why was C-cot born?" The narrator explains that "Cecot was built following the declaration of a state of emergency, with mass arrests taking place," necessitating "a vast facility... to house the criminals." As an aerial night view of the massive, blue-lit, grid-like CECOT complex is shown, the narrator emphasizes it is "the largest prison in Latin America, spanning the size of eight football fields with a capacity of 40,000 inmates." A map of El Salvador then highlights the country's location, with animated markers for "Barrio 18 and MS-13," which the narrator identifies as "two of the largest gangs in Latin America" now "behind bars." Chaotic scenes of Tattooed inmates being subdued by Masked prison guards in a corridor, with one inmate running bare-chested, visually reinforce the prison's purpose. The Man in red t-shirt is later seen walking through the prison grounds, speaking to the camera, while the Guard walks ahead, with the Man noting, "Very few people are granted permission. I might be the first Arab to receive permission to film inside." He then asks, "How difficult is to escape this prison?" The narrator responds, "Impossible," as the segment ends with a close-up of a watchtower and barbed wire fencing, detailing the prison is "surrounded by massive, 15-meter high concrete walls, reinforced with barbed wire and 19 watchtowers." Arabic and English subtitles provide additional context throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a narrator stating that escaping from the CECOT prison is impossible due to its extreme security, guarded by 600 soldiers and 250 police officers, as the Man in red t-shirt is escorted by a Masked prison guard along a paved outdoor path under a clear blue sky. They pass a high-security building with a watchtower and barbed wire, then enter a covered walkway, proceeding down a brightly lit corridor with blue-painted lower walls, passing other guards. The narrator announces, 'We're going now to the armory,' echoing on-screen text in Arabic and English. The guard leads the man to a room marked 'ARMERÍA' (Armory) under the CECOT logo. Inside the sterile, well-lit armory, rows of black rifles are displayed on metal racks. The narrator continues, 'This is an armory here,' warning that anyone thinking of escaping should 'just look at what awaits them,' and mentions El Salvador declared war on gangs. As the guard demonstrates a firearm to the man, who gestures and asks questions, the narrator reiterates, 'Here we are in the weapons warehouse.' Background music intensifies, and a distinct voice (possibly a guard or another narrator) states, 'So you are announce a war against the gang,' followed by, 'Yes, we have this other type of weaponry,' as close-ups show the rifles and a table with handcuffs and chains, emphasizing the armory's function and the prison's strict security protocols.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside the CECOT prison's weapons warehouse, where a Masked prison guard, in a black uniform, shows a Man in red t-shirt various armaments. A male voice, likely the guard, explains that these 'weapons' are part of a 'war on terrorism' to make the country safer, emphasizing the need to 'have them under control.' The guard points out different restraints, including 'cuffs' for 'hands' and 'leg cuffs' for 'feet,' demonstrating how 'these bind both hands and feet together,' causing individuals to 'walk uncomfortably.' The Man in red t-shirt listens intently, examining handcuffs and chains, reacting with surprise as the guard demonstrates a large metal chain. The guard then holds a baton, identified by the voice as 'The Tonfa.' Tense, atmospheric music begins as the scene transitions to the man standing between two heavily armored officers in riot gear, whose 'equipment' is explained as 'designed for emergencies' and a 'result of the state of emergency,' serving to protect citizens as 'public safety is the priority.' The group exits the armory, walking through a corridor and into the prison yard. The camera follows them to a vehicle, and the perspective shifts to a first-person view from inside a car. The vehicle drives along a road towards the modern, high-security prison complex, passing a watchtower and barbed wire fencing, with the distinct sound of the engine and road noise accompanying the journey. The drive continues past the prison’s white and blue exterior, under bright, sunlit skies with distant mountains, before arriving at a covered area where the man is greeted by another guard, as a phone rings in the background and the music fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in red t-shirt, wearing a 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' t-shirt, ascending concrete stairs outside a modern, industrial-style building marked 'COMEDOR AGENTES'. He is escorted by a Masked prison guard in a black uniform, cap with 'JUSTICIA', and 'CECOT'/'EL SALVADOR' insignia. The guard's voice explains that the facility, including the large, empty dining hall they enter, is "for the guards, not the inmates," adding that "the prisoners cannot leave their cells." As they converse, with Arabic subtitles translating dialogue, the guard asks the man if he feels "responsible of the safety of the country," to which the man replies, "Yes, this is what makes a country safe," before thanking the guard for his service. The man then walks through a covered outdoor corridor with a mirror and a wall-mounted fan, briefly checking his reflection, as the guard's voice clarifies that even the shaving facilities are "for the guards" who "must be well taken care of since they're surrounded by some of the most dangerous criminals in the world." They proceed down a long, sterile hallway past a 'COCINA' door and enter a dormitory filled with metal bunk beds. The guard's voice identifies these as "the guards' dormitories," explaining their "five days on, five days off" schedule designed "to minimize the impact of stress." The man picks up a dumbbell and engages in a light workout, while the guard continues to detail the environment, highlighting the CECOT prison's consistently bright, institutional, clean, organized, and secure nature.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside a well-lit gym with various exercise equipment, where a Man in red t-shirt is seen lifting dumbbells. A Masked prison guard in black uniform, who serves as the speaker, explains that "This gym is for the guards. It's all for the guards' well-being," implying it helps them cope "from being there in front of those criminals all day." As background music begins, the man and the guard exit the building, walking along a sunlit outdoor path toward a high-security facility. The speaker invites, "Come with me. Now we're about to enter the prisoners' area. This is the most important part of the video." They pass a chain-link gate topped with razor wire, labeled 'ZONA 2 MÓDULOS 3-4 CECOT', indicating the Center of Confinement of Terrorism. The guard leads the man through the gate, with the speaker emphasizing that "The world's most dangerous gang leaders are just a few steps away," and that "Once they enter the Center of Confinement of Terrorism, they will never leave again. Once they pass through, through this gate, they never come out." The man points toward a large white building with a curved roof, and the camera pans to show guards on an upper balcony. The scene transitions to the man being escorted into a covered corridor with a sign reading 'MÓDULO 4 CECOT', then into a long, brightly lit prison hallway lined with barred cells, accompanied by the distinct sound of a gate. The speaker states, "We're now going to the cells," and then, "Come inside. We walked into the gangs..." The hallway is marked with a sign 'CELDAS' above the entrance, and the man walks down the corridor, greeting guards with 'Good evening' as he passes. Other guards and inmates are visible in the background, some standing near cell doors, while the man continues walking, observing the structured and controlled, high-security environment of the CECOT prison.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a long, brightly lit prison corridor, featuring a curved ceiling with linear lighting, yellow floor markings, and barred cells on both sides, some marked with numbers like '7' and '31'. A man in a red t-shirt, with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on his back, walks down the hallway, flanked by numerous masked prison guards in black uniforms. As he proceeds, the man greets the surroundings with a clear "Buenas tardes!", which is met by a collective "Buenas tardes!" response, likely from the guards and inmates. Inside the cells, numerous tattooed inmates, wearing white uniforms, T-shirts, white shorts, and white slippers—described by the narrator as their only possessions—are seen standing or sitting, observing with intense gazes. The narrator explains that none of their family members are allowed to visit, and that these inmates, from different gangs, are imprisoned together, having participated in the murder of over 200 people, rape, and many other crimes. Close-ups reveal their detailed tattoos on heads and arms, some inmates resting on metal surfaces, as the narrator notes they sleep on these iron surfaces due to a lack of mattresses. The narrator further describes that if one looks at them, they will give a "terrifying glare," and emphasizes the "huge number of guards" present, making it feel "like the country is at war." The man in red continues walking, occasionally turning to look at the inmates, while the guards maintain a vigilant presence in this modern, high-security environment, all prisoners uniformly dressed in white. Throughout, a low ambient hum and subtle, tense background music underscore the controlled and high-stakes atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of the brightly lit, high-security CECOT prison corridor, featuring a curved ceiling, linear lighting, and barred cells. The guard, speaking with an accent, emphasizes that "Control and monitoring is 24/7" and that a camera system allows real-time monitoring of all activities, a statement reinforced by Arabic and English subtitles. A narrator's voice further details the strict regimen, explaining that the lights are never turned off, staying on "24/7" so "prisoners never know the time and the guards can monitor them," preventing misbehavior. Close-ups reveal Tattooed inmates inside the cells, many wearing blue surgical masks and displaying intricate head tattoos, some looking intensely at the camera. The narrator adds that each cell, despite a capacity of 100, only has 80 beds, and inmates are denied blankets. The scene shifts back to the corridor as the Man in red t-shirt continues his tour, listening intently. The camera then focuses on a group of Masked prison guards in gray uniforms and balaclavas conducting a security check on inmates inside a cell, using handcuffs and performing physical checks amidst faint clanking sounds. The inmates, in white uniforms and masks, sit or stand behind bars, some with visible arm and leg tattoos, as guards methodically secure them. The narrator clarifies that "prisoners are only allowed out of their cells for 30 minutes a day to exercise, using only their body weight." The constant low hum of the facility underscores the tense, highly structured atmosphere, with yellow floor lines marking pathways and guards stationed at intervals, emphasizing the prison's strict control.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a stark close-up of heavily tattooed inmates, their bodies intricately adorned with names, dates, and symbolic imagery like skulls and women’s faces, seated on the floor of a brightly lit prison corridor. A voiceover immediately explains that "their tattoos made it easier to identify and capture them," noting that an "18 tattoo" signifies a "Barrio 18 member." The camera pans across the group, revealing their shaved heads and white prison uniforms, while masked prison guards in gray shirts and black balaclavas stand vigilantly. A man in a red t-shirt, bearing 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' on his back, walks through the corridor, gesturing and conversing with a guard in a black uniform. The voiceover continues, detailing that "most of the tattoos... have some tombstones," worn "in memory of other gang members, members of their clique who were eliminated," and also include "tattoos of graves and skulls of people they've killed." As the man in red points to specific tattoos, a close-up reveals detailed artwork, including a tombstone with 'Santana Guzmán' and 'R.I.P.', which the voiceover identifies as "his grandmother." Another tattoo is identified as "his son's face." The guard in black, holding a clipboard, inspects the tattoos. The voiceover further clarifies that these tattoos are "in memory of fallen gang members," often depicted as skulls, and "every tattoo has a meaning." The modern, high-security facility, with barred cells marked '19' and '13' and a yellow demarcation line, maintains a somber and controlled atmosphere. The segment concludes with the man in red continuing his conversation, the inmates remaining serious and reflective, as a female vocal track begins to play, and the voiceover abruptly states, "They arrested them all and locked..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a brightly lit, high-security CECOT prison corridor, where a line of tattooed inmates in white uniforms are seated on the floor. A Man in a red t-shirt, identified by 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' on his back, walks through, accompanied by a Guard in a black uniform. The Man in red t-shirt provides continuous commentary, explaining that these inmates belong to two gangs that were once at war but are now imprisoned together. As the Guard points out specific tattoos, the Man in red t-shirt describes common designs like women's faces on their legs, skulls, and marijuana imagery, which are also highlighted by Arabic subtitles. The camera pans across the inmates, revealing extensive tattoos including names like 'Santana Guzmán'. Ominous, low synth music begins, underscoring the tense atmosphere as the Man in red t-shirt expresses personal apprehension, stating, 'Thankfully I live far away. If any of them got a hold of me in the future, I'd be in serious trouble.' He continues his tour, reiterating that these former enemy gangs now share the same cells. The scene shifts to barred cell doors, showing more tattooed inmates, some with light blue hair, standing in formation. The Man in red t-shirt gestures towards the cells, emphasizing the scale and the harsh reality that 'Their entire lives are confined within this cell. They can never leave. This is their toilet,' pointing out its location in the corner. Close-ups reveal individual inmates, including one with a somber expression, accompanied by Arabic subtitles translating to 'and later murdered her.' The segment concludes with a wide shot of the controlled prison environment, with the Man in red t-shirt continuing his commentary and tour amidst the vigilant masked prison guards.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in red t-shirt, identified as the narrator, speaking into a microphone in front of a barred cell at CECOT prison, explaining that inmates have been there for two years since the prison opened and all criminals were transferred there. He gestures toward Tattooed inmates behind bars, many shirtless and displaying extensive tattoos, including names like 'Santana Guzmán' and symbolic imagery. The narrator continues, explaining that Masked prison guards cover their faces out of fear that inmates might contact outsiders for revenge on their families. The scene transitions to a wide shot of a prison corridor as the Man in red t-shirt walks down the hall, flanked by Masked prison guards, passing Tattooed inmates sitting cross-legged on the floor, some with visible handcuffs and chains. He gestures toward the camera, then proceeds down the corridor, stating they are heading to the solitary confinement cells where any prisoner who misbehaves ends up. He is escorted by a Guard in black uniform toward a heavy metal door marked 'CECOT 5', which opens with a distinct sound to reveal a dark, bare Cell interior. The narrator describes it as having 'no light' and 'only a cement slab.' The Man in red t-shirt enters, holding a flashlight, and gestures to the sparse interior, emphasizing the minimal conditions, with the narrator adding that misbehaving prisoners are placed there for 15 days. He then exits and walks down another corridor, passing a cell with a yellow bucket and a sink. Arabic subtitles, translating to 'and later murdered her,' appear during a close-up of a somber inmate. The scene shifts to a dining area where a Worker in a hairnet pushes a Food distribution cart of stacked plastic containers, handing them through the bars to inmates. The narrator explains that inmates 'don't even leave their cells for meals' and 'food is delivered inside.' Close-ups show the Food container being distributed—containing 'beans and rice, no chicken, no meat'—into individual containers, with inmates reaching through the bars. The environment is stark, with cold lighting and a clinical atmosphere, underscored by faint, somber background music, emphasizing the institutional nature of the prison.

The interview concludes with the inmate looking down, as an English voiceover summarizes his confession: 'He says he cries every day and regrets what he has done, but it's too late. He feels remorse.'

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens inside a stark, dimly lit prison cell where a man in a red t-shirt faces a somber inmate in white prison garb, flanked by two masked guards. A narrator states, "but he must face justice," as the man in red t-shirt asks the inmate, "So when he start enter the gang, like how old he was?" Arabic subtitles translate the question, and the inmate responds in Spanish, "Como la de 11 años," which is translated on screen as '11 years old'. The narrator then emphasizes, "He joined the gang when he was just 11 years old, and now he is here in CECOT." Gentle, reflective background music begins as the scene shifts to an aerial view of the modern CECOT prison complex. The man in red t-shirt is then seen walking through its sunlit corridors, arms outstretched, passing a guard, while the narrator praises President Nayib Bukele's "bold and brilliant" actions, noting his 2019 approval rating was "around 50%." A political montage follows, highlighting a map of El Salvador in 2019 with Bukele's portrait and '53.1% of the vote'. Bukele is then shown speaking from the Palacio Nacional balcony at night, declaring in English, "Today, El Salvador has broken all the records," to a cheering crowd, with Arabic subtitles and triumphant music. The map reappears, showing 2024 election results with '64.65% of the vote', though the narrator claims Bukele "won by a landslide with 85% of the vote." Bukele is then heard declaring El Salvador "the safest country in the entire Western Hemisphere" amidst falling confetti, with the sound of confetti audible. The narrator explains, "Why? Because for the first time, the people felt safe." The video cuts to children smiling in a sunny park, symbolizing safety, as the narrator concludes, "This is the end of our tour of CECOT prison. What did you learn from this episode? There's a lot we can learn from this president." The scene returns to the man in red t-shirt walking through the CECOT prison corridor, approaching a guard at a doorway with a golden sunset behind him, accompanied by the sound of a door and a final musical flourish.
